title: Athletic Training (BOC) Practice Question Bank
description: Board of Certification style practice questions for athletic training, organized by the 5 practice-analysis domains. Extracted via OCR from source screenshots and expanded with pattern-matched generated items.

questions.stem: When crossing multiple time zones, what can be done to help combat jet lag?
options: Consume copious amounts of caffeine only when traveling east
Adjust all schedules to the local time once you arrive
Consume copious amounts of caffeine only when traveling west
Make no changes to your schedules
correct_answer: Adjust all schedules to the local time once you arrive
questions.explanation: The most effective way to reset the circadian rhythm and combat jet lag is to immediately adopt the destination's local time for sleeping, eating, and activity. Caffeine loading is not a recommended primary strategy.

questions.stem: To prevent the possibility of an acute compartment syndrome, the athletic trainer should advise his or her field hockey players to wear what piece of equipment?
questions.explanation: Acute compartment syndrome in field hockey typically results from a direct blow to the lower leg (e.g., from a ball or stick). Shin guards protect the anterior lower leg, reducing the risk of the contusion and swelling that can precipitate an anterior compartment syndrome.

questions.stem: Which organization certifies football helmets to make sure they are able to withstand repeated blows of high mass and low velocity?
questions.explanation: The National Operating Committee on Standards for Athletic Equipment (NOCSAE) establishes and certifies the safety standards for football helmets and other protective sports equipment.

questions.stem: True or False: A very low-carb diet can cause extremely high blood pH levels.
questions.explanation: A very low-carbohydrate (ketogenic) diet increases ketone bodies (acids), tending to LOWER blood pH toward acidosis, not raise it. High pH (alkalosis) is not a consequence of a low-carb diet.

questions.stem: A flak jacket is a piece of equipment designed to protect the athlete after sustaining what type of injury?
questions.explanation: A flak jacket is a padded protective vest worn over the thorax to shield the ribs and allow an athlete to return to play after a rib contusion or fracture.

questions.stem: A copious dose of the following vitamin is potentially very dangerous?
questions.explanation: Vitamin A is fat-soluble and accumulates in the body, so excessive intake can cause serious toxicity (hypervitaminosis A). The water-soluble vitamins listed (B6, C, B12) are largely excreted, making acute toxicity far less likely.

questions.stem:
You are aware of the general use of smokeless tobacco among your high school baseball players and decide to develop a seminar for the coaches and players to discourage it. What are common effects of chronic chewing tobacco use?

I. Leukoplakia
II. Mouth and throat cancer
III. Increased aggression
IV. Bad breath
V. Mood swings
VI. Gynecomastia
questions.explanation: Chronic smokeless (chewing) tobacco use commonly causes leukoplakia (white oral lesions), oral/throat cancer, and bad breath. Increased aggression, mood swings, and gynecomastia are not characteristic effects, so the combination I, II, IV is correct.

questions.stem: Which type of athlete is most susceptible to heatstroke, therefore, must be carefully educated regarding the importance of adequate fluid intake during activity in hot, humid weather?
questions.explanation: A defensive lineman has the largest body mass and wears heavy, insulating equipment, reducing heat dissipation and greatly increasing susceptibility to exertional heatstroke in hot, humid conditions.

questions.stem: Where do primitive stem cells mature into red or white blood cells and platelets?
questions.explanation: Hematopoiesis—the maturation of primitive stem cells into red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets—occurs in the red bone marrow in adults.

questions.stem: Your female athlete is showing signs of low bone mass. How much calcium should she consume per day?
questions.explanation: For a female athlete showing signs of low bone mass (as with amenorrhea/female athlete triad), the recommended calcium intake is elevated to approximately 1500 mg per day, above the standard 1000-1200 mg baseline, to help protect bone density.

questions.stem: Fibroids of the uterus always develop into cancer.
questions.explanation: Uterine fibroids (leiomyomas) are benign smooth-muscle tumors; they very rarely, if ever, transform into cancer. The absolute word "always" makes the statement false.

questions.stem: The flexor pollicis longus is responsible for which action(s)?
questions.explanation: The flexor pollicis longus flexes the thumb, specifically the interphalangeal and metacarpophalangeal joints of the pollex (thumb). It is not primarily an adductor or abductor of the thumb.

questions.stem: Heel cups come in two designs. Which one has an elevated heel that can compress downward so that the heel do not make contact with the interior aspect of the shoe?
questions.explanation: The rigid (hard plastic) heel cup design elevates and cups the heel so that it compresses downward and does not contact the interior of the shoe, off-loading the painful area; the other design is a soft cushioned cup that simply pads. The rigid plastic cup matches the description.

questions.stem: What should the athletic trainer advise his or her athletes to do to minimize the negative effects of jet lag?
options: Drink an adequate amount of fluids, especially water, to avoid dehydration
Keep watches set to their “home” time zone
Get up and go to bed 2 to 3 hours earlier for each time zone crossed
Wear warm clothing to avoid getting cold while on the plane
Get at least 8 hours of sleep at night the week before the flight
correct_answer: Drink an adequate amount of fluids, especially water, to avoid dehydration
questions.explanation: Staying well hydrated by drinking adequate fluids (especially water) counteracts the dehydrating effects of dry cabin air and is a proven measure to reduce jet lag symptoms. Watches should be reset to local (destination) time, and shifting sleep by 2-3 hours per time zone is excessive.

questions.stem: The cure rate for skin cancer exceeds 95% with early detection and treatment.
questions.explanation: With early detection and treatment, skin cancers (particularly basal and squamous cell carcinomas) have very high cure rates exceeding 95%. The stray 'V' in the stem is an OCR artifact.

questions.stem: What are sensory receptors located at the musculotendinous junction, which monitor active tension generated by the muscle during a contraction, called?
questions.explanation: Golgi tendon organs are located at the musculotendinous junction and detect changes in muscle tension during contraction, triggering autogenic inhibition to protect against excessive force. Muscle spindles, by contrast, monitor changes in muscle length.

questions.stem:
Terminology – Leg Alignment – Fill in the Blanks

Fill in the blanks with the correct terminology.

____: Patella is more superior than normal while standing.
____: Patella is more inferior than normal while standing.
____: Patella is situated more medial than normal, also known as knock-knees.
____ Patella is situated more lateral than normal, also known as bow-legs.
____: Knees are hyperextended when standing
options: Patella alta (Patella is more superior than normal while standing)
Patella baja (Patella is more inferior than normal while standing)
Genu valgum (Patella is situated more medial than normal, also known as knock-knees)
Genu varum (Patella is situated more lateral than normal, also known as bow-legs)
Genu recurvatum (Knees are hyperextended when standing)
questions.explanation: The correct answers, confirmed by the green-highlighted answer key in the image: a superiorly positioned patella is patella alta; an inferiorly positioned patella is patella baja; medial deviation (knock-knees) is genu valgum; lateral deviation (bow-legs) is genu varum; and hyperextended knees are genu recurvatum. These are standard lower-extremity postural/alignment terms.

questions.stem: One of your female patients presents with dysuria, a sense of urgency to urinate, decreased urine volume, nocturia, low back pain, and pyuria. What do you suspect this patient has?
questions.explanation: Dysuria, urinary urgency and frequency, nocturia, suprapubic/low back pain, and pyuria are the classic presentation of cystitis (bladder infection), a common lower urinary tract infection in women. Prostatitis is impossible in a female, and the other options do not fit these urinary symptoms.

questions.stem: What is Myopia?
questions.explanation: Myopia is nearsightedness, in which the eye focuses light in front of the retina so distant objects appear blurred while near objects are seen clearly. (Farsightedness is hyperopia.)

questions.stem: Cerebral Palsy is diagnosed by skeletal muscle biopsy.
questions.explanation: Cerebral palsy is a clinical diagnosis based on history, developmental delay, and neurologic examination (often supported by neuroimaging such as MRI), not by skeletal muscle biopsy. Muscle biopsy is used for primary muscle diseases like muscular dystrophy.

questions.stem: If a patient has purulent discharge in the eye, what do you believe their diagnosis is?
questions.explanation: Thick, purulent (pus-like) discharge is the hallmark of bacterial conjunctivitis, whereas viral/allergic conjunctivitis produces watery discharge. Corneal abrasion and macular degeneration do not cause purulent discharge.

questions.stem: Upon visual inspection, an athletic trainer observes an athlete's fifth digit in extension at the MCP and DIP joints and flexion at the PIP joint. Which injury has this athlete sustained?
questions.explanation: A boutonniere deformity presents as PIP joint flexion with DIP (and MCP) hyperextension, resulting from disruption of the central slip of the extensor tendon.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old baseball player has been diagnosed with acute appendicitis. Upon evaluation, what signs and symptoms might he be complaining of?
questions.explanation: Acute appendicitis classically presents with periumbilical pain migrating to the right lower quadrant with rebound tenderness and abdominal rigidity, low-grade fever, nausea/vomiting, anorexia, constipation, and leukocytosis. Options such as cyanosis, deviated trachea, jaundice, Kehr's sign, and coffee-ground emesis point to other conditions. Marked medium confidence because the option list continues below the fold and exact intended selections cannot be fully confirmed.

questions.stem:
You are covering a women's basketball game at your school, when you see one of your athletes go up for a rebound and land awkwardly on her right leg. She falls to the court in obvious pain so you run onto the court to tend to the athlete.

You do your preliminary evaluation and escort her to the athletic training facility, which is next to the basketball court. You suspect she has torn her ACL. Which of the following special tests is used to test the integrity of the ACL?
questions.explanation: The Lachman's test is the most sensitive and reliable special test for anterior cruciate ligament integrity. McMurray's and Apley's test the meniscus, the varus stress test assesses the LCL, and the posterior drawer tests the PCL.

questions.stem: What type of muscular contraction by the quadriceps help decelerate the body during running descending hills?
questions.explanation: During downhill running the quadriceps lengthen under tension to control and decelerate the body, which is an eccentric contraction.

questions.stem: Which four tests will you recommend along with your evaluation?
questions.explanation: For a cardiovascular evaluation, the standard first-line diagnostic tests are electrocardiography, echocardiography, cardiac stress testing, and cardiac catheterization. Marked medium confidence because the scenario stem is incomplete and an option may be cut off at the bottom of the screenshot, so the intended four cannot be fully verified.

questions.stem:
Balance and coordination are critical for athletic performance.

What is the feedback from the muscles, as to what they are doing during a particular activity, known as?

What is the area of the brain that assists in controlling movement?
questions.explanation: Proprioception is the sensory feedback from muscles, tendons, and joints about body position and movement, and the cerebellum is the primary brain region coordinating and refining movement, balance, and coordination.

questions.stem: The basal metabolic rate is increased in individuals with hyperthyroidism.
questions.explanation: Thyroid hormone drives metabolism; in hyperthyroidism excess hormone raises the basal metabolic rate, producing symptoms such as weight loss, heat intolerance, and tachycardia.

questions.stem: What parameter is less essential to check while evaluating the neurovascular status of an injured limb?
options: The pulse distal to the injury
questions.explanation: This stem asks for the least essential parameter. Neurovascular assessment centers on the neural (sensation, motor function) and vascular (distal pulses) status of the limb. Joint range of motion is a musculoskeletal parameter and is the least essential to the specific neurovascular check.

questions.stem: An athlete collides with a teammate during an ice hockey game and sustains a blow to the jaw with a hockey stick. He is holding his lower face with his hands and is in intense pain. What are not appropriate measures when the athletic trainer is evaluating the athlete to determine he has a fractured jaw?
options: Have the athlete bite and observe for a malocclusion
Palpate the jaw for a hematoma
Ask the athlete to open and close his mouth noting asymmetry with movement
Palpate for jaw deformity when it is at rest, open, and closed
See if the athlete can retract his jaw and observe for difficulty with movement
correct_answer: See if the athlete can retract his jaw and observe for difficulty with movement
questions.explanation: This is a negatively worded stem. Standard mandible fracture assessment includes checking for malocclusion, palpating for hematoma/deformity, and observing open/close movement. Testing jaw retraction is not a standard or appropriate evaluation measure for a suspected mandibular fracture.

questions.stem: What is not an antihistamine drug?
questions.explanation: Ultram (tramadol) is a centrally acting opioid analgesic, not an antihistamine. Benadryl (diphenhydramine), Claritin (loratadine), Allegra (fexofenadine), and Dimetapp (which contains an antihistamine) are all antihistamine products.

questions.stem:
A 40-year-old male presents with a history of a chronic, productive cough lasting for more than three months over the past two to three years. He appears short of breath and does not tolerate moderately long periods of exercise. He is a smoker.

Which two diagnoses do you believe he might be suffering from?
questions.explanation: A chronic productive cough for at least three months over two or more consecutive years in a smoker is the diagnostic definition of chronic bronchitis, which together with emphysema comprises COPD. The dyspnea and exercise intolerance in this smoker support both diagnoses.

questions.stem: What organ is effected if the athlete presents with jaundice?
questions.explanation: Jaundice (yellowing of the skin and sclera) results from elevated bilirubin, most commonly reflecting liver dysfunction in processing and excreting bilirubin. The liver is the primary organ implicated.

questions.stem: Upon visual inspection, an athletic trainer observes an athlete’s fifth digit in extension at the MCP and DIP joints and flexion at the PIP joint. Which injury has this athlete sustained?
questions.explanation: Boutonniere deformity is characterized by flexion of the PIP joint with hyperextension of the DIP (and MCP) joint, resulting from disruption of the central extensor tendon slip. This matches the described posture of the fifth digit.

questions.stem: An athlete sustains a blunt force trauma injury to the upper right quadrant of their abdomen. What structure in this area is the most likely to be injured?
questions.explanation: The liver occupies most of the right upper quadrant and is the structure most likely to be injured by blunt trauma there. The spleen is in the left upper quadrant.

questions.stem: One of your patients begins to complain of intense itching under the cold pack that you applied to his shoulder five minutes ago. When you remove the cold pack, you notice that hives have developed on the area that has been in contact with the cold pack. What is the [...]ter, used to describe what patient is experiencing?
questions.explanation: Cold urticaria is a hypersensitivity reaction in which cold exposure produces itching, redness, and hives (wheals) on the contacted skin, exactly as described. It is a known contraindication to cryotherapy.

questions.stem: Graves' disease, systemic lupus erythematosus, and rheumatoid arthritis are all autoimmune diseases.
questions.explanation: Graves' disease (autoimmune hyperthyroidism), systemic lupus erythematosus, and rheumatoid arthritis are all autoimmune disorders in which the immune system attacks the body's own tissues, so the statement is true.

questions.stem: What diagnosis does EIA stands for?
questions.explanation: EIA is the standard abbreviation for exercise-induced asthma (also called exercise-induced bronchoconstriction), a transient airway narrowing triggered by physical exertion.

questions.stem: During CPR, it is most convenient and efficient to monitor the athlete's circulation by palpating the carotid artery. Where is the carotid pulse located?
options: In the groove between the larynx and the sternocleidomastoid muscle
Between the mastoid process and pharynx
At the base of the neck superior to the manubrium
2 inches below the base of the earlobe on the side of the neck
correct_answer: In the groove between the larynx and the sternocleidomastoid muscle
questions.explanation: The carotid pulse is palpated in the groove between the trachea/larynx and the sternocleidomastoid muscle by sliding the fingers laterally from the larynx. The antecubital fossa is the brachial pulse site.

questions.stem: An athlete comes to the athletic training facility with a tick embedded in his lower leg. What should the athletic trainer instruct the athlete to do? Select all that apply.
options: Keep the area clean and free of other debris
Pull the tick off with tweezers
Cover the tick with mineral oil
Put fingernail polish remover onto its body
correct_answer: Keep the area clean and free of other debris
Pull the tick off with tweezers
questions.explanation: Proper tick removal is to grasp the tick as close to the skin as possible with fine tweezers and pull straight out with steady pressure, then clean the area. Folk methods such as smothering with mineral oil or applying nail polish remover are not recommended, as they can cause the tick to regurgitate and increase infection risk.

questions.stem: A patient comes to the athletic training facility with a tick embedded in his lower leg. What should the athletic trainer instruct the patient not to do?
options: Keep the area clean and free of other debris
Pull the tick off with tweezers
Cover the tick with mineral oil
Put fingernail polish remover onto its body
correct_answer: Cover the tick with mineral oil
questions.explanation: Ticks should be removed with fine-tipped tweezers by grasping close to the skin and pulling straight out; smothering methods such as mineral oil, petroleum jelly, nail polish, or heat are discouraged because they can cause the tick to regurgitate and increase infection risk. Because the stem is negatively worded and 'fingernail polish remover' is also inappropriate, confidence is medium given the single-answer format; mineral oil (a classic smothering method) is the intended answer.

questions.stem: What is an AED used for?
options: To provide immobilization of the lumbar spine and pelvis after a spinal injury
To treat arrhythmias of the heart
To assess the athlete's level of consciousness after a head injury
To defibrillate the heart when an unconscious victim has no pulse
correct_answer: To defibrillate the heart when an unconscious victim has no pulse
questions.explanation: An automated external defibrillator delivers a shock to restore a normal rhythm in a pulseless, unconscious victim experiencing a shockable arrhythmia (ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia). It is used during cardiac arrest, not for general arrhythmia treatment in a conscious patient.

questions.stem: A patient should be referred to a physician after a concussion when there is a loss of consciousness on the field, amnesia lasting longer than 15 minutes, deterioration of neurologic function, unequal, dilated, or unreactive pupils, irregularity in respirations, and/or vomiting.
questions.explanation: These are all classic red-flag indicators warranting immediate physician referral (and often emergency transport) following a concussion, as they suggest significant or worsening intracranial pathology.

questions.stem: Deep frostbite is considered a medical emergency. What is the proper course of treatment for a patient that has frostbite?
options: Gradual rewarming of the body part with warm water.
Firm, sustained pressure of the hand on the affected body part.
Rapid rewarming of the affected body part with warm water.
Quick friction in order to increase local circulation
correct_answer: Rapid rewarming of the affected body part with warm water.
questions.explanation: Deep frostbite is treated with rapid rewarming in circulating warm water (approximately 100-104 degrees F / 37-40 degrees C). Friction/rubbing is contraindicated because ice crystals cause further tissue damage, and gradual rewarming is less effective and risks refreezing.

questions.stem: One of your patients has been stung by a bee and is showing signs of anaphylaxis. Which medication would be appropriate to administer to stop the reaction?
questions.explanation: Epinephrine is the first-line, life-saving medication for anaphylaxis; it reverses bronchoconstriction and vasodilation. Heparin (anticoagulant), antitussives (cough suppressants), and H2 blockers (acid reducers) do not treat acute anaphylaxis.

questions.stem:
A 15-year-old lacrosse player gets checked during a game and receives a hard blow to his forearm. You run onto the field, and when you begin your secondary survey, you notice he is in severe pain and is grabbing at his arm. You see that there is a gross deformity of the proximal/dorsal aspect of the forearm. He complains he cannot rotate his forearm.

The athlete is in obvious discomfort. After your initial assessment, what do you do next?
options: Splint the upper arm and forearm to support and protect the extremity
Give the athlete a sling to wear
Strap the athlete's arm to his side
Give the athlete Naprosyn for pain
Give the athlete Percocet for pain
correct_answer: Splint the upper arm and forearm to support and protect the extremity
questions.explanation: A suspected forearm fracture with gross deformity should be immobilized by splinting the joints above and below the injury to support and protect the extremity before transport. Athletic trainers do not administer prescription analgesics like Naprosyn or Percocet on the field.

questions.stem: Under which conditions should an athletic helmet be removed after an injury?
options: If the athlete requests it be removed
If, after a reasonable period of time, the facemask cannot be removed to gain access to the airway
The chief EMT requests that it be removed
If the helmet causes immobilization of the head or jaw
The helmet should never be removed
correct_answer: If, after a reasonable period of time, the facemask cannot be removed to gain access to the airway
questions.explanation: A properly fitted helmet should generally be left in place to maintain spinal alignment; removal is indicated if the airway cannot be accessed after the facemask cannot be removed in a reasonable time, if the helmet does not hold the head securely, or if it prevents proper immobilization.

questions.stem: Anterior compartment syndrome may be addressed without consequence up to 72 hours after the initial injury.
questions.explanation: Acute anterior compartment syndrome is a surgical emergency; irreversible muscle and nerve damage begins within 6-8 hours, so fasciotomy must be performed promptly. Waiting 72 hours would cause permanent tissue necrosis.

questions.stem: What is considered a medical emergency?
questions.explanation: Acute compartment syndrome is a true surgical emergency; rising intracompartmental pressure compromises circulation and can cause irreversible tissue and nerve damage within hours, requiring immediate fasciotomy. The other conditions are serious but not acute, life-or-limb-threatening emergencies.

questions.stem: An athlete is brought into the athletic training facility with a 2.5-inch nail embedded in his foot. What is not an appropriate treatment for this injury?
options: Pack the nail and foot as they are in a large dressing to help control the bleeding and stabilize the object
Transport the athlete to the hospital with the nail still embedded in the foot
Verify that the athlete's tetanus shot is current
questions.explanation: An impaled object should never be removed in the field because it may be tamponading a vessel; removal can cause severe hemorrhage and further tissue damage. The object should be stabilized in place and the athlete transported with it embedded.

questions.stem: An athlete comes to the athletic trainer holding a tooth that has just been knocked out of his mouth. What are the proper steps for the athletic trainer to take to allow for a successful reimplantation?
options: Try to put it back in place and use a topical ointment for pain
Put the tooth in a jar of water and tell the athlete to store it in a refrigerator until he can see a dentist
There is nothing the athletic trainer can do because the tooth is dead
Place the tooth in a cloth soaked with saline or water and get the athlete to a dentist within 30 minutes.
correct_answer: Place the tooth in a cloth soaked with saline or water and get the athlete to a dentist within 30 minutes.
questions.explanation: An avulsed tooth must be kept moist (ideally in Hank's solution, milk, or saline) and reimplanted as quickly as possible, as prognosis drops sharply after about 30 minutes. Of the choices given, transporting the tooth moist to a dentist within 30 minutes is the appropriate action.

questions.stem:
A 19-year-old basketball player was hit in the right eye during a rebound. He complains of pain, diplopia, and photophobia

Upon inspection, the athletic trainer notes the following:
– Subconjunctival redness
– Orbital erythema
– Unequal pupils
– Swelling over the zygomatic arch and inferior orbit
– Partial contact lens is seen in the eye
– Excessive tearing

Based on observation of the athlete's eye, what treatments should the athletic trainer complete at this time?
options: Apply an ice compress to the athlete's eye
Evaluate visual acuity with Snellen eye chart
Place the athlete in a recumbent position
Place the athlete in a supine position with elevated legs
Refer the athlete to physician/emergency room
correct_answer: Apply an ice compress to the athlete's eye
Evaluate visual acuity with Snellen eye chart
Refer the athlete to physician/emergency room
questions.explanation: Signs of an orbital/blowout fracture with globe involvement (unequal pupils, diplopia, zygomatic/orbital swelling) warrant applying ice to control swelling, assessing baseline visual acuity, and urgent physician/ER referral. A fluorescein stain is contraindicated when a contact lens is present and globe rupture is possible, and leg elevation is for shock, not an isolated eye injury.

questions.stem: What is the prudent method of transporting an athlete with a suspected spinal injury?
options: Do not move the athlete until a doctor is present
Use a spine board with medical assistance
correct_answer: Use a spine board with medical assistance
questions.explanation: An athlete with a suspected spinal injury must be immobilized and transported on a spine board using a trained team to maintain in-line spinal stabilization and prevent secondary neurological injury.

questions.stem: How many athletic trainers should be utilized to transport an athlete off the field by manual conveyance?
questions.explanation: Manual conveyance (assisting a mobile, conscious athlete off the field) is performed by two athletic trainers, one on each side supporting the athlete under the arms. This is used only when spinal or lower-extremity injury has been ruled out.

questions.stem: When evaluating an unconscious athlete, what is the athletic trainers first step?
options: Check that the athlete’s airway is open and he or she is breathing normally
correct_answer: Check that the athlete’s airway is open and he or she is breathing normally
questions.explanation: In the primary survey of an unconscious athlete, establishing an open airway and adequate breathing is the first priority (airway/breathing precede other assessments).

questions.stem: A male patient sustains a direct blow to the genital area. How can the athletic trainer assist him to immediately decrease the pain?
options: Use an ice pack to the area with the patient in supine, knees in extension
Use direct pressure on the testes
Have the patient lie supine with his knees bent to his chest to decrease the strain on the scrotum and apply ice to the affected area
Have the patient lie on his side with his knees extended
Place a moist heat pack over the area
correct_answer: Have the patient lie supine with his knees bent to his chest to decrease the strain on the scrotum and apply ice to the affected area
questions.explanation: For a blow to the testes, having the athlete flex the knees toward the chest relaxes the abdominal/cremasteric muscles and reduces scrotal strain, and applying ice helps control pain and swelling.

questions.stem: What are contraindications for iontophoresis?
questions.explanation: Contraindications for iontophoresis include impaired circulation/ischemia, arterial (peripheral vascular) disease, known sensitivity or allergy to the medication being delivered, and damaged or broken skin/open lesions over the treatment site. Analgesia and inflammation are not contraindications (inflammation is often an indication).

questions.stem:
Which are contraindications to massage?

I. Inflammation
II. Pregnancy
III. Hemorrhage
IV. Infection
V. Phlebitis
questions.explanation: Acute inflammation, active hemorrhage, infection, and phlebitis (with risk of dislodging a thrombus) are all contraindications to massage. Pregnancy per se is not an absolute contraindication (only certain techniques/areas are cautioned), so the option excluding II (I, III, IV, V) is best.

questions.stem: When fitting an athlete for crutches, what angle should the elbow be bent to?
questions.explanation: Proper axillary crutch fit places the elbow in approximately 25-30 degrees of flexion when the hands are on the handgrips, allowing effective push-off while keeping weight off the axilla.

questions.stem:
One of your softball players is a Type 1 diabetic. You are preparing for an all-day softball tournament, and your athlete reports to you that her blood glucose level is 90 mg/dL.

The athlete notices a blister is forming on the back of her right heel. What do you do next?
options: Inspect the area and make sure it is properly cared for to avoid any complications
Wash it with an alcohol-based solution to dry it out
Have the athlete change shoes and wear white socks
Tell the athlete to suck it up and put an adhesive bandage on it
correct_answer: Inspect the area and make sure it is properly cared for to avoid any complications
questions.explanation: In a diabetic athlete, even a minor blister carries elevated risk of poor healing and infection, so the appropriate first action is to inspect the area and ensure it is properly cared for to prevent complications. Alcohol drying, simply changing socks, or dismissing the injury are inadequate or harmful in this population.

questions.stem: What is an appropriate treatment for an acute quadriceps contusion?
options: Ice massage followed by gentle stretch
Ice pack with compression wrap with the knee in flexion
correct_answer: Ice pack with compression wrap with the knee in flexion
questions.explanation: For an acute quadriceps contusion, ice and compression with the knee held in flexion limits hematoma formation and helps maintain range of motion, reducing the risk of myositis ossificans. Massage and heat are contraindicated acutely.

questions.stem: Which instructions or techniques are correct for the use of a cane?
options: Cane Height is adjusted so elbow is flexed 20-30 degrees
Cane height is adjusted to the ASIS
Cane is held in the contralateral hand
Cane is held in ipsilateral hand
Cane moves forward with the involved extremity
Cane moves forward with the uninvolved extremity
correct_answer: Cane Height is adjusted so elbow is flexed 20-30 degrees
Cane is held in the contralateral hand
Cane moves forward with the involved extremity
questions.explanation: A cane is fitted with the elbow flexed about 20-30 degrees (top at the greater trochanter/wrist crease), held in the hand OPPOSITE (contralateral to) the involved leg, and advanced together with the involved/affected extremity to widen the base of support and offload that limb.

questions.stem: An athlete is 5 days post-op from a partial meniscectomy. What exercise is not appropriate to perform at this time?
questions.explanation: This stem is negatively phrased ("not appropriate"). At only 5 days post partial meniscectomy the early rehab focus is on range of motion, edema control, and gentle isometric/low-load activity; aggressive eccentric quadriceps strengthening imposes high loads too soon and is inappropriate. The other options (Achilles stretch, straight leg raises, low-resistance cycling, hip flexor work) are appropriate early-phase activities.

questions.stem: What is the normal dosage for one tablet of Aspirin?
questions.explanation: The standard adult dose of one regular-strength aspirin tablet is 325 mg.

questions.stem: Which modality should be utilized if the desired therapeutic effect is decreased pain, edema and inflammation?
questions.explanation: Cryotherapy (ice packs) produces vasoconstriction and decreased metabolic activity, which reduces pain, edema, and inflammation. The other listed modalities are thermal/heating agents that increase circulation.

questions.stem: Which is not an example of an isotonic device?
questions.explanation: Isotonic exercise involves movement against a constant resistance through a range of motion (free weights, total gym, squat rack, wall pulleys). Pushing against an immovable wall produces no movement and is an isometric exercise, not an isotonic device.

questions.stem: Conventional TENS use what frequency in the pulses per second range with what phase duration in µseconds?
questions.explanation: Conventional (high-rate) TENS uses a high frequency of roughly 50 to 100 pulses per second with a short phase duration of about 2 to 50 microseconds. The image shows this option selected.

questions.stem: When performing an interval throwing program, the progression of throwing distances of 45 feet, 60 feet, 90 feet, 120 feet, 150 feet, and 180 feet are appropriate provided there is no increased pain or soreness the next day.
questions.explanation: The standard interval throwing program progresses through graduated distances (45, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180 feet), advancing only when the athlete completes a level without increased pain or next-day soreness.

questions.stem: When rehabilitating a patient who has undergone an ACL reconstruction, which muscle groups must be strengthened to support the graft?
questions.explanation: The hamstrings are agonists to the ACL; they resist anterior tibial translation and protect the graft, so hamstring strengthening is emphasized in ACL reconstruction rehabilitation. (Quadriceps strengthening is also important but is progressed cautiously to avoid anterior shear on the graft.)

questions.stem: What is one of the most serious adverse reactions during iontophoresis treatment?
questions.explanation: Galvanic (chemical/electrical) burns are one of the most serious adverse reactions of iontophoresis, resulting from the direct current altering skin pH beneath the electrodes, most often an alkaline burn under the cathode.

questions.stem: When rehabilitating a cervical strain, what must the athletic trainer maintain the integrity of?
questions.explanation: The cervical musculature is functionally linked to the shoulder girdle (trapezius, levator scapulae, etc.), so cervical strain rehabilitation must maintain the strength and integrity of the shoulder girdle to restore proper neck function and posture.

questions.stem: When is the best time to begin a rehabilitation program after an injury?
options: After the inflammation is under control
questions.explanation: Rehabilitation should begin immediately after injury; early controlled intervention (within the limits of the healing tissue) minimizes deconditioning and optimizes recovery. The stray 'V' in the stem is an OCR artifact.

questions.stem: What is not a contraindication for using cryotherapy?
questions.explanation: Inflammation is an INDICATION for cryotherapy (cold reduces inflammation, pain, and metabolic demand), not a contraindication. Raynaud's phenomenon, cold urticaria, and vasculitis are all contraindications to cold application.

questions.stem: What is not a factor in designing an appropriate treatment program?
options: How soon the coach feels the athlete should return to play
correct_answer: How soon the coach feels the athlete should return to play
questions.explanation: A treatment/rehabilitation program is designed on clinical criteria (stage of tissue healing, pain with mobility, severity of injury), not on a coach's desired return-to-play timeline, which is a non-clinical pressure that should not dictate the program.

questions.stem: What is a type of organizational culture characterized by consensus, teamwork, and participatory decision making?
questions.explanation: A collegial organizational culture emphasizes consensus, teamwork, shared governance, and participatory decision making among members, in contrast to hierarchical or formulistic structures.

questions.stem: What is not an error when planning the design of a sports medicine facility?
options: Concentrating on function rather than style
The use of wood for the main flooring material
Having the adaptive access on the other side of the building
Non-ground fault interrupter receptacles places within the hydrotherapy area
Having only one entrance and exit
correct_answer: Concentrating on function rather than style
questions.explanation: This is a negatively worded stem asking which choice is NOT a design error. Prioritizing function over style is the correct approach to facility design, whereas wood flooring, distant/inaccessible ADA access, non-GFCI receptacles near water, and a single entrance/exit are all recognized planning errors.

questions.stem: Which organization certifies facemasks that are used in ice hockey helmets?
questions.explanation: The Hockey Equipment Certification Council (HECC) certifies ice hockey helmets and facemasks in the United States, verifying they meet the applicable ASTM performance standards.

questions.stem: When a patient is injured and there is an abrupt withdrawal from training, this can lead to a condition known as sudden exercise abstinence syndrome.
questions.explanation: Sudden exercise abstinence syndrome refers to the psychological and physiological disturbances (e.g., mood changes, anxiety, depression) that can occur when a highly active person abruptly stops training due to injury. This is a recognized concept in the psychology of injury.

questions.stem: During a pre-participation examination, an athlete reveals that they had surgery during the summer that may affect their sports participation status. The athletic trainer contacts the surgeon to learn the details of the surgery, but is denied access to the athlete's records. What federal legislation is responsible for protecting the athletes medical information?
options: Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act
Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act
correct_answer: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act
questions.explanation: HIPAA governs the privacy and release of protected health information held by covered entities such as physicians; the surgeon cannot release records without proper authorization. FERPA applies to educational records, not a physician's medical records.

questions.stem: You are in the position to hire an assistant athletic trainer. You have a specific person in mind, but your athletic director implies he is not comfortable with this person's gender. Which level of the law mandates the hiring and firing of employees?
questions.explanation: Employment discrimination based on protected classes such as sex/gender is governed at the federal level, primarily under Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 enforced by the EEOC. Federal law sets the baseline mandate for lawful hiring and firing practices.

questions.stem: Which two pieces of documentation should be used to record this information?
questions.explanation: SOAP (Subjective, Objective, Assessment, Plan) and HOPS (History, Observation, Palpation, Special tests) are the two standard formats used to document a clinical injury evaluation. The prior context referenced by 'this information' is not shown in the screenshot, so confidence is medium.

questions.stem: Using a cookbook approach for clinical practice that ignores clinician experience or the circumstances of a specific patient is acceptable as long you utilize the same treatment on all patients of similar injuries.
questions.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates the best available research evidence with clinician expertise and individual patient values/circumstances; a rigid 'cookbook' approach that ignores clinician experience and patient-specific factors is not acceptable.

questions.stem: Which completed form is used in order for an athletic trainer to gain the quickest reimbursement from an insurance company?
questions.explanation: The HCFA-1500 (now CMS-1500) is the standard universal claim form used to bill and obtain reimbursement from insurance companies for professional healthcare services.

questions.stem: The codes that were established by the AMA for health care providers to receive third-party reimbursement are CPT codes. What does CPT stand for?
questions.explanation: CPT stands for Current Procedural Terminology, the standardized code set maintained by the American Medical Association for reporting medical procedures and services for third-party reimbursement.

questions.stem: Emotional instability, depression, a reduced sense of accomplishment, and a lack of interest in activity or sport are all potential signs of a patient suffering from burnout.
questions.explanation: Burnout in athletes is characterized by emotional exhaustion/instability, depression, a reduced sense of accomplishment, and a decreased interest or devaluation of the sport. All listed signs are recognized indicators of burnout.

questions.stem: Which methods for funding public sector sports medicine facilities utilizes an institution’s assets in cash and investments that are not normally used for operational purposes?
questions.explanation: An endowment is a fund of cash and invested assets held by an institution whose principal is not used for day-to-day operational purposes; earnings support designated needs. This matches the description in the stem.

questions.stem: What documents are essential for the treatment of participants who are minors?
options: Current medical progress notes from a licensed physician
Current physical examination results from a licensed physician
Permission/consent for medical treatment from a parent of guardian
Written assumption of risk signed by parent or guardian
correct_answer: Current physical examination results from a licensed physician
Permission/consent for medical treatment from a parent of guardian
Written assumption of risk signed by parent or guardian
questions.explanation: For minors, essential documents include a current pre-participation physical examination, parental/guardian consent for medical treatment (since minors cannot legally consent), and a signed assumption of risk. Routine medical progress notes are not a prerequisite document for treatment.

questions.stem: Which of the following are performance evaluation standards intended to help foster practicality in the employee appraisal process?
questions.explanation: Among the four personnel evaluation standard categories (Propriety, Utility, Feasibility, Accuracy), the Feasibility standards specifically address practicality, ensuring appraisals are realistic, efficient, and viable to implement.

questions.stem:
You have been given permission to hire a new staff athletic trainer. What order should these components follow when conducting staff selection?

I. Retention
II. Hiring
III. Promotion
IV. Performance evaluation
V. Demotion
questions.explanation: The sequence must begin with Hiring (II). Of the listed options, only 'II, III, I, IV, V' starts with Hiring, so it is the best available answer among the verbatim choices. Confidence is lowered because the ideal HR order (Hiring, Retention, Performance evaluation, Promotion/Demotion) is not perfectly represented.

questions.stem: A wrestler comes to the athletic training facility inquiring about a “drug that will make him lose weight quickly,” mentioning a diuretic. Which drugs has been banned by the International Olympic Committee that are diuretics?
questions.explanation: Furosemide is a loop diuretic banned by the IOC/WADA, both for use as a masking agent and for rapid weight loss. Salbutamol is a beta-2 agonist and caffeine is a stimulant, neither of which is a diuretic.

questions.stem: Job description, hiring practices, vacation policies, benefits, and release of medical records are all of human resources issues.
questions.explanation: Job descriptions, hiring practices, vacation policies, benefits, and confidentiality/release of records all fall under human resources management within an athletic training organization/administration context, so the statement is true.

questions.stem: If a patient, who is covered by a HMO insurance policy, needs to see a specialist, who must make the referral?
questions.explanation: Under an HMO model, the primary care physician acts as the gatekeeper and must provide the referral before the patient can see a specialist and have the visit covered.

questions.stem: What is the term used for a primary care physician appointed by an insurance company to oversee the medical care given to a patient and assigns any specialty or ancillary services?
questions.explanation: In managed care (e.g., HMO models), the primary care physician who coordinates a patient's care and authorizes referrals to specialists and ancillary services is termed the gatekeeper.

questions.stem: What type of supplies include such items as adhesive tape, gauze pads, and bandages?
questions.explanation: Expendable supplies are consumable, single-use items that cannot be reused and must be continually replenished, such as adhesive tape, gauze pads, and bandages. (Nonexpendable/capital items are durable and reusable.)

questions.stem: Evidence-based practice is best defined as the use of the consensus of scientific evidence to indicate the best clinical practices for managing specific conditions.
questions.explanation: Evidence-based practice is not defined solely by scientific consensus; it is the integration of the best available research evidence with clinical expertise and the individual patient's values and circumstances. Defining it only as the consensus of scientific evidence is incomplete/incorrect.

questions.stem: According to the wet bulb globe temperature (WBGT) index, which environmental factor is weighted most heavily because it best reflects the body's ability to dissipate heat through evaporation?
questions.explanation: The standard WBGT formula weights the natural wet bulb temperature at 70%, because high humidity limits evaporative cooling (sweat) and is the primary driver of exertional heat illness risk.

questions.stem: When fitting a football helmet, which of the following indicates a properly fitted helmet?
options: The front rim sits at the level of the eyebrows and the ear holes align with the ear canals
The front rim rests two finger-widths below the eyebrows
The helmet can be rotated freely on the head when the chin strap is fastened
The jaw pads leave a visible gap against the face for airflow
The helmet is one size larger than measured to allow for a skull cap
correct_answer: The front rim sits at the level of the eyebrows and the ear holes align with the ear canals
questions.explanation: A properly fitted football helmet sits approximately one inch (about two finger-widths) above the eyebrows with ear holes aligned to the ear canals, snug jaw pads, and no rotation when the chin strap is secured.

questions.stem: A closed basket-weave ankle taping technique is primarily intended to limit which motion to reduce the risk of a lateral (inversion) ankle sprain?
questions.explanation: The classic lateral ankle sprain mechanism is plantarflexion combined with inversion; stirrups and heel locks in a basket-weave application restrict this motion to protect the anterior talofibular and calcaneofibular ligaments.

questions.stem: Which macronutrient recommendation is most appropriate for an endurance athlete seeking to maximize muscle glycogen replenishment within the first 30 to 60 minutes after prolonged exercise?
options: Approximately 1.0 to 1.2 g of carbohydrate per kilogram of body weight
Approximately 0.1 g of carbohydrate per kilogram of body weight
A high-fat meal of at least 60% calories from fat
correct_answer: Approximately 1.0 to 1.2 g of carbohydrate per kilogram of body weight
questions.explanation: Consuming roughly 1.0-1.2 g/kg of carbohydrate in the immediate post-exercise window takes advantage of elevated glycogen synthase activity and GLUT-4 translocation to rapidly restore glycogen stores.

questions.stem: During a preparticipation physical examination, which finding is considered a red flag that most warrants further cardiac evaluation before clearance?
options: Exertional syncope or a family history of sudden cardiac death before age 50
A benign functional (flow) murmur that decreases with standing
Mild resting sinus bradycardia in a well-trained distance runner
A single episode of muscle cramping after a summer practice
correct_answer: Exertional syncope or a family history of sudden cardiac death before age 50
questions.explanation: Exertional syncope and a family history of premature sudden cardiac death are key warning signs (per AHA screening elements) that may indicate hypertrophic cardiomyopathy or other conditions and require referral before participation.

questions.stem: Which muscle is the primary agonist responsible for hip flexion and is commonly targeted in flexibility programs to reduce anterior pelvic tilt?
questions.explanation: The iliopsoas (iliacus and psoas major) is the primary hip flexor; tightness pulls the pelvis into anterior tilt, so it is a common focus of flexibility and stretching interventions.

questions.stem: An athletic trainer is educating athletes about acclimatization for a preseason held at high altitude. Which physiologic adaptation occurs to improve oxygen delivery during prolonged altitude exposure?
options: Increased erythropoietin secretion leading to increased red blood cell production
Decreased ventilatory rate to conserve oxygen
Reduced capillary density in skeletal muscle
Decreased resting heart rate within the first 24 hours
correct_answer: Increased erythropoietin secretion leading to increased red blood cell production
questions.explanation: Hypoxia at altitude stimulates the kidneys to release erythropoietin, increasing red blood cell mass over days to weeks and improving the oxygen-carrying capacity of the blood.

questions.stem: True or False: A properly fitted mouthguard should cover the maxillary (upper) teeth and extend to the posterior molars, helping to reduce the risk of dental injury and, potentially, concussive forces transmitted through the jaw.
questions.explanation: A well-fitted mouthguard covers the upper teeth to the last molars, absorbing and dispersing impact forces to protect the dentition and reduce transmission of force to the temporomandibular joint and cranium.

questions.stem: True or False: Ballistic stretching, which uses bouncing movements to force a joint beyond its normal range, is the preferred method for a general pre-activity warm-up because it minimizes the risk of activating the muscle stretch reflex.
questions.explanation: Ballistic stretching actually triggers the myotatic (muscle spindle) stretch reflex and increases injury risk; dynamic stretching is preferred for pre-activity warm-up, while static stretching is generally reserved for after activity.

questions.stem: Which of the following are recognized signs or symptoms of exertional heat exhaustion? (Select all that apply.)
options: Heavy sweating with pale, clammy skin
Core temperature typically below 104 degrees Fahrenheit (40 degrees Celsius)
Hot, dry skin with an absence of sweating
Persistent central nervous system dysfunction such as combativeness and coma
correct_answer: Heavy sweating with pale, clammy skin
Core temperature typically below 104 degrees Fahrenheit (40 degrees Celsius)
questions.explanation: Heat exhaustion presents with profuse sweating, cool/clammy skin, dizziness, nausea, and a core temperature usually under 104 degrees F. Hot dry skin and marked CNS dysfunction are hallmarks of exertional heat stroke, a medical emergency.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old cross-country runner reports to the athletic trainer during a July practice when the WBGT reads 30 degrees Celsius (86 degrees Fahrenheit). She is confused, staggering, has hot skin, and a rectal temperature of 105.8 degrees Fahrenheit (41 degrees Celsius). Which is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Begin cold-water immersion to rapidly lower core temperature and then activate EMS
Have her sit in the shade, sip a sports drink, and monitor for 30 minutes before deciding
Apply ice bags to the forehead only and send her home with a parent
Encourage her to walk it off slowly to promote circulation
Administer an antipyretic such as acetaminophen and continue practice
correct_answer: Begin cold-water immersion to rapidly lower core temperature and then activate EMS
questions.explanation: This presentation (CNS dysfunction plus core temperature above 104 degrees F) is exertional heat stroke. The standard of care is immediate whole-body cold-water immersion to cool first, then transport (cool first, transport second), as rapid cooling within 30 minutes is critical to survival.

questions.stem: What are documented health consequences associated with chronic smokeless (chewing) tobacco use? I. Leukoplakia of the oral mucosa II. Increased risk of oral and pharyngeal cancer III. Gingival recession and periodontal disease IV. Nicotine dependence and elevated resting heart rate
questions.explanation: Chronic smokeless tobacco use is linked to all four: leukoplakia, oral/pharyngeal cancer, gingival recession and periodontal disease, and nicotine dependence with cardiovascular effects such as elevated heart rate and blood pressure.

questions.stem: During evaluation of a wrestler's hand, an athletic trainer observes persistent flexion of the distal interphalangeal (DIP) joint of the ring finger, with the athlete unable to actively extend the tip after the finger was caught in an opponent's jersey. Which injury has this athlete most likely sustained?
questions.explanation: Jersey finger is a rupture of the flexor digitorum profundus tendon at its distal insertion, classically occurring when a finger is caught in a jersey. The athlete loses active flexion of the DIP joint. Mallet finger, by contrast, involves loss of active DIP extension due to extensor tendon avulsion.

questions.stem: An athletic trainer is evaluating an athlete who reports intermittent right upper quadrant abdominal pain that worsens after eating fatty meals, sometimes radiating to the right shoulder. Which organ is the most likely source of this referred pain pattern?
questions.explanation: Cholelithiasis (gallstones) classically produces right upper quadrant pain that worsens after fatty meals and refers to the right shoulder/scapula via the phrenic nerve. Splenic pathology typically refers to the left shoulder (Kehr's sign).

questions.stem: A soccer player is struck on the lateral aspect of the knee. During evaluation, the athletic trainer performs a test by applying a valgus force while flexing and internally rotating the tibia, then extending the knee, feeling for a clunk of reduction. This special test is designed to assess which structure?
questions.explanation: The pivot shift test evaluates anterolateral rotatory instability from ACL insufficiency. Applying a valgus force with internal rotation causes anterior subluxation of the tibia, which reduces with a palpable clunk as the knee is flexed past ~30 degrees.

questions.stem: In a patient with untreated type 1 diabetes mellitus experiencing diabetic ketoacidosis, a fruity (acetone) odor on the breath and deep, rapid Kussmaul respirations are expected clinical findings.
questions.explanation: Diabetic ketoacidosis produces ketone bodies that give the breath a fruity/acetone odor, and the resulting metabolic acidosis triggers compensatory Kussmaul respirations (deep, rapid breathing) to blow off CO2.

questions.stem: Blanchable erythema that does not resolve and progresses to a shallow open ulcer with partial-thickness skin loss over a bony prominence is consistent with a Stage 1 pressure injury.
questions.explanation: A Stage 1 pressure injury presents as intact skin with non-blanchable erythema. Partial-thickness skin loss with a shallow open ulcer describes a Stage 2 pressure injury.

questions.stem: An athletic trainer inspects a lesion on a lacrosse player's forearm consisting of honey-colored crusts over an erythematous base, spreading among teammates in the locker room. Which condition is most consistent with these findings?
questions.explanation: Impetigo, most often caused by Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus pyogenes, produces the hallmark honey-colored (golden) crusting and is highly contagious, spreading easily in athletic settings. Tinea presents as annular scaling plaques and herpes gladiatorum as grouped vesicles.

questions.stem: A distance runner presents to the athletic training room reporting a red, painful eye with the sensation of a foreign body, excessive tearing, and photophobia after being poked during a fall. Fluorescein staining under a cobalt-blue light reveals a bright green uptake area on the cornea. This finding indicates which condition?
questions.explanation: Fluorescein dye pools in areas of disrupted corneal epithelium and fluoresces green under cobalt-blue light, confirming a corneal abrasion. A hyphema is blood in the anterior chamber, and a subconjunctival hemorrhage is painless blood beneath the conjunctiva without staining.

questions.stem: While reviewing a basketball player's medication list, an athletic trainer sees the athlete has been prescribed albuterol via metered-dose inhaler. This medication is most commonly used to manage which condition?
questions.explanation: Albuterol is a short-acting beta-2 agonist (bronchodilator) used as a rescue inhaler for asthma and exercise-induced bronchoconstriction. Recognizing the drug class helps identify an athlete's underlying respiratory condition.

questions.stem: An athletic trainer palpates the anatomical snuffbox of a gymnast who fell on an outstretched hand and reports wrist pain. Point tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox should raise suspicion for a fracture of which bone?
questions.explanation: The scaphoid forms the floor of the anatomical snuffbox, so tenderness there after a fall on an outstretched hand is highly suggestive of a scaphoid fracture. This fracture warrants imaging and immobilization due to the risk of avascular necrosis from its retrograde blood supply.

questions.stem: A wrestler reports painless, gross hematuria (bright red urine) after a match but denies flank pain, dysuria, or recent trauma. Which of the following is the LEAST likely benign explanation and should prompt referral for further workup?
questions.explanation: Exercise-induced hematuria, bladder contusion, beeturia, and myoglobinuria are relatively benign or self-limiting causes of red urine in athletes. Painless gross hematuria can be a red-flag presentation of a urinary tract malignancy and warrants referral, especially if it persists after rest.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old volleyball player collapses during practice. Bystanders report she had complained of chest palpitations and lightheadedness. She has a family history of a relative who died suddenly at a young age. Select TWO cardiac conditions that are most important to consider as causes of sudden cardiac death in a young athlete.
options: Mitral valve prolapse with mild regurgitation
questions.explanation: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is the most common structural cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes and often has a familial pattern. Commotio cordis (ventricular fibrillation from a blunt chest blow during a vulnerable cardiac cycle) is a leading non-structural cause. The other options are far less likely to cause sudden death in this population.

questions.stem: A 21-year-old cross-country runner reports a 3-week history of fatigue, unintentional weight loss, increased thirst, and frequent urination. During evaluation the athletic trainer notes the athlete has been drinking large volumes of water and using the restroom far more than usual. A random capillary blood glucose reading is 312 mg/dL. Which condition should the athletic trainer suspect and refer for immediate physician evaluation?
questions.explanation: The classic triad of polyuria, polydipsia, and unexplained weight loss combined with a markedly elevated random blood glucose (>=200 mg/dL with symptoms) strongly suggests new-onset diabetes mellitus. This requires prompt physician referral. Diabetes insipidus causes polyuria/polydipsia but with normal blood glucose.

questions.stem: During the primary survey of a collapsed athlete who is unresponsive, which finding takes priority for immediate intervention?
options: Absence of a carotid pulse and normal breathing
Report of neck stiffness from a teammate
correct_answer: Absence of a carotid pulse and normal breathing
questions.explanation: The primary survey addresses life threats in order of circulation, airway, and breathing. Absence of pulse and normal breathing indicates cardiac arrest and requires immediate CPR, taking priority over any orthopedic or soft-tissue finding.

questions.stem: An athletic trainer applies an automated external defibrillator (AED) to a pulseless, non-breathing athlete. Which action is NOT appropriate during AED use?
options: Ensuring no one is touching the athlete while the device analyzes the rhythm
Continuing chest compressions until the pads are attached
Pouring water on the chest to improve pad adhesion on a sweaty athlete
Resuming compressions immediately after a shock is delivered
Removing a medication patch from the pad placement site
correct_answer: Pouring water on the chest to improve pad adhesion on a sweaty athlete
questions.explanation: A wet chest should be quickly dried, not made wetter, because moisture can conduct current across the skin surface and reduce shock effectiveness while posing a hazard. All other options reflect correct AED technique.

questions.stem: An athlete sustains an arterial bleed from a deep thigh laceration that continues after firm direct pressure. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Elevate the limb and wait for clotting
Apply a commercial tourniquet proximal to the wound
Remove the saturated dressing and inspect the wound
Apply pressure to the popliteal pulse point
correct_answer: Apply a commercial tourniquet proximal to the wound
questions.explanation: When direct pressure fails to control life-threatening extremity hemorrhage, a tourniquet applied proximal to the wound is the current standard of care. Dressings should never be removed once saturated; additional dressings are layered on top.

questions.stem: Which set of signs is most consistent with a patient progressing into hypovolemic shock?
options: Flushed skin, slow bounding pulse, and elevated blood pressure
Pale, cool, clammy skin with a rapid, weak pulse and falling blood pressure
Warm dry skin, constricted pupils, and normal pulse
Redness at the injury site with localized swelling only
Slow deep respirations with a strong regular pulse
correct_answer: Pale, cool, clammy skin with a rapid, weak pulse and falling blood pressure
questions.explanation: Hypovolemic shock produces peripheral vasoconstriction and compensatory tachycardia, yielding pale, cool, clammy skin, a rapid thready pulse, and a late drop in blood pressure.

questions.stem: A football athlete is suspected of a cervical spine injury while wearing a helmet and shoulder pads. Which action is NOT appropriate?
options: Maintaining manual in-line stabilization of the head and neck
Removing the helmet while leaving the shoulder pads in place
Removing the face mask to access the airway
Log-rolling the athlete as a unit with adequate personnel
Securing the athlete to a spine board with the head immobilized
correct_answer: Removing the helmet while leaving the shoulder pads in place
questions.explanation: Removing the helmet but leaving the shoulder pads on forces the cervical spine into hyperextension. The helmet and shoulder pads should be managed together as a unit; if removed, both come off together.

questions.stem: An athlete collapses during a distance race on a hot, humid day with an altered mental status and a rectal temperature of 106 degrees F. What is the priority emergency action?
options: Transport immediately to the hospital before cooling
Give oral fluids and rest in the shade
Begin cold-water immersion to rapidly lower core temperature
Apply cold packs to the forehead only
correct_answer: Begin cold-water immersion to rapidly lower core temperature
questions.explanation: Exertional heat stroke is a medical emergency where cold-water immersion should begin immediately on site following the cool-first, transport-second principle. Rapid cooling within the first minutes is the strongest predictor of survival.

questions.stem: An athlete is struck in the mouth and a permanent tooth is completely avulsed. Which is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Scrub the root thoroughly with gauze to remove debris
Store the tooth in dry gauze and refer to a dentist within a week
Gently rinse the tooth and, if possible, reimplant it into the socket or store it in milk
Discard the tooth and apply pressure to the socket
Place the tooth in ice water for preservation
correct_answer: Gently rinse the tooth and, if possible, reimplant it into the socket or store it in milk
questions.explanation: An avulsed permanent tooth should be handled by the crown only, gently rinsed without scrubbing, and reimplanted promptly. If reimplantation is not possible, it should be stored in milk or a tooth-preservation solution to maintain viability of the periodontal cells.

questions.stem: When an object is impaled in an athlete's forearm, the athletic trainer should stabilize the object in place and leave it in the wound rather than removing it.
questions.explanation: Impaled objects should be stabilized and left in place because removal can cause severe hemorrhage and further tissue damage. The exception is an object obstructing the airway.

questions.stem: An athlete with a known bee-sting allergy who develops hives, facial swelling, and difficulty breathing should have epinephrine administered via auto-injector without delay.
questions.explanation: These signs indicate anaphylaxis, a life-threatening reaction. Intramuscular epinephrine is the first-line treatment and should be given immediately, followed by activation of emergency medical services.

questions.stem: An athlete has an open, bleeding wound on the lower leg. Select ALL of the appropriate immediate wound-care actions.
options: Apply direct pressure with a sterile dressing
Wear appropriate personal protective equipment such as gloves
Remove the initial dressing repeatedly to check the wound
Cover the wound with a sterile dressing once bleeding is controlled
Apply a tourniquet as the first measure for a minor bleed
correct_answer: Apply direct pressure with a sterile dressing
Wear appropriate personal protective equipment such as gloves
Cover the wound with a sterile dressing once bleeding is controlled
questions.explanation: Appropriate wound care includes universal precautions with PPE, direct pressure with a sterile dressing, covering the controlled wound, and monitoring for shock. Repeatedly removing the dressing disrupts clot formation, and a tourniquet is reserved for uncontrolled life-threatening hemorrhage, not minor bleeds.

questions.stem: A basketball player falls and lands on an outstretched hand, presenting with an obvious deformity and pain at the mid-forearm but with a strong radial pulse and normal finger sensation. After confirming the athlete is stable, what should the athletic trainer do next?
options: Attempt to realign the deformity before splinting
Splint the forearm in the position found, immobilizing the joints above and below the injury
Have the athlete continue play with a wrap
Apply traction and reduce the fracture
Remove the athlete's shoe to check circulation
correct_answer: Splint the forearm in the position found, immobilizing the joints above and below the injury
questions.explanation: With distal circulation and sensation intact, the deformity should be splinted in the position found while immobilizing the joints above and below the fracture site. Athletic trainers do not reduce fractures in the field.

questions.stem: A wrestler is struck in the eye and reports blurred vision. On inspection, the athletic trainer sees blood pooling in the anterior chamber (hyphema). What is the most appropriate management?
options: Apply firm pressure to the globe and continue competition
Irrigate the eye with saline and return the athlete to play
Shield the eye without pressure, keep the athlete upright, and refer for emergency evaluation
Attempt to remove the blood from the eye with gauze
Patch both eyes tightly and have the athlete lie flat
correct_answer: Shield the eye without pressure, keep the athlete upright, and refer for emergency evaluation
questions.explanation: A hyphema is an ophthalmologic emergency. The eye should be protected with a rigid shield applying no pressure to the globe, the athlete kept upright to help blood settle, and immediate referral arranged to prevent further vision loss.

questions.stem: Which modality is MOST appropriate during the acute inflammatory phase to reduce metabolic demand and limit secondary hypoxic injury?
questions.explanation: During the acute inflammatory phase, cryotherapy lowers tissue temperature and metabolic rate, which limits secondary hypoxic (enzymatic) cell death and reduces edema. The other listed modalities produce heat, which is contraindicated in acute injury.

questions.stem: When fitting an athlete for axillary crutches, approximately how much space should remain between the top of the crutch pad and the axilla?
options: The width of the entire hand
questions.explanation: A gap of roughly 1 to 2 inches (two to three finger widths) prevents the athlete from bearing weight through the axilla, which would risk compression of the brachial plexus and axillary vessels ('crutch palsy'). Weight should be borne through the hands.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT an example of isokinetic exercise?
options: Performing a leg curl with free weights
correct_answer: Performing a leg curl with free weights
questions.explanation: Isokinetic exercise uses a dynamometer to hold angular velocity constant while accommodating resistance throughout the range. Free weights provide a fixed (isotonic) load, so a free-weight leg curl is not isokinetic.

questions.stem: Therapeutic ultrasound intended to reach deep tissue with minimal thermal effect (nonthermal, cavitation-driven) is typically delivered at which frequency and duty cycle?
questions.explanation: A 1 MHz frequency penetrates deeper tissue (up to about 5 cm), and a pulsed 20% duty cycle produces nonthermal effects by minimizing heat buildup. A 3 MHz frequency targets superficial tissue, and 100% (continuous) output is thermal.

questions.stem: Conventional (high-rate, sensory-level) TENS for gate-control pain modulation typically uses which pulse frequency and phase duration?
questions.explanation: Conventional TENS uses a high frequency (50 to 100 pulses per second) with a short phase duration (about 50 to 100 microseconds) to stimulate large-diameter sensory afferents and produce rapid, gate-control analgesia. Low-rate/acupuncture-like TENS uses low frequencies with longer durations.

questions.stem: In iontophoresis, dexamethasone (a negatively charged anti-inflammatory) is driven into the tissue from which electrode?
options: Either electrode, polarity does not matter
questions.explanation: Iontophoresis relies on like charges repelling. Because dexamethasone is negatively charged, it is placed under and driven from the negative electrode (cathode), since like charges repel and push the ion into the tissue.

questions.stem: Which proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation (PNF) technique is used primarily to INCREASE range of motion by contracting the agonist to reciprocally inhibit the tight antagonist?
questions.explanation: Slow-reversal-hold-relax uses a contraction of the agonist muscle to produce reciprocal inhibition of the tight antagonist, allowing greater range of motion. Rhythmic stabilization emphasizes co-contraction for stability rather than ROM gains.

questions.stem: Isometric exercise produces muscle tension without a change in muscle length or visible joint movement, making it useful early in rehabilitation when joint motion must be limited.
questions.explanation: Isometric contractions generate tension while joint angle and muscle length stay constant. Because there is no joint movement, they are commonly used early after injury or surgery to maintain strength while protecting healing tissue.

questions.stem: Therapeutic ultrasound may be safely applied directly over the anterior neck, eyes, and a pregnant uterus as long as the intensity is kept low.
questions.explanation: These are absolute contraindications for ultrasound regardless of intensity. Ultrasound should never be applied over the eyes, reproductive organs, a pregnant uterus, the carotid sinus/anterior neck, the heart, or a pacemaker.

questions.stem: Which of the following are recognized contraindications to the application of a thermal modality such as a moist heat pack? (Select all that apply.)
options: Acute injury within the first 24 to 48 hours
Peripheral vascular disease with poor circulation
A subacute muscle strain with residual stiffness
correct_answer: Acute injury within the first 24 to 48 hours
Peripheral vascular disease with poor circulation
questions.explanation: Heat is contraindicated over acute/bleeding injuries because it promotes vasodilation and increased edema/bleeding, and over areas of impaired sensation or compromised circulation because of burn risk. A subacute strain with residual stiffness is actually an appropriate indication for heat.

questions.stem: A soccer athlete is 4 weeks post-op after ACL reconstruction with a patellar tendon autograft. She has full passive extension, roughly 110 degrees of flexion, minimal effusion, and good quad control with a straight-leg raise and no extension lag. Which intervention is MOST appropriate to add at this stage of the rehabilitation progression?
options: Closed-kinetic-chain exercises such as mini-squats and leg press within a protected 0 to 60 degree range
Open-chain isokinetic knee extension at high speed through full range
Sport-specific cutting and pivoting drills at full speed
Prolonged immobilization in a locked extension brace with no exercise
Aggressive open-chain resisted knee extension from 90 to 45 degrees
correct_answer: Closed-kinetic-chain exercises such as mini-squats and leg press within a protected 0 to 60 degree range
questions.explanation: At about 4 weeks with good quad control and full extension, progressing to protected closed-kinetic-chain strengthening is appropriate because it minimizes anterior shear/strain on the healing graft. Full-range open-chain extension and cutting/pivoting are introduced much later once strength and stability criteria are met.

questions.stem: An athlete presents with a deep vein thrombosis (DVT) in the calf. Which of the following modalities/techniques is absolutely CONTRAINDICATED in this region?
options: Intermittent pneumatic compression / vigorous massage over the calf
Cryotherapy to an unrelated shoulder injury
Isometric exercise of the uninvolved arm
correct_answer: Intermittent pneumatic compression / vigorous massage over the calf
questions.explanation: Mechanical compression or vigorous massage over a known DVT can dislodge the clot and cause a pulmonary embolism, so it is absolutely contraindicated. DVT is a medical emergency requiring physician referral; unrelated-region activity is not contraindicated.

questions.stem: In the SOAP note format used for medical documentation, which section contains the athletic trainer's clinical impression and diagnosis based on the gathered information?
questions.explanation: In a SOAP note, the Assessment (A) section records the clinician's professional judgment, clinical impression, or working diagnosis derived from the subjective and objective findings. The Plan section then outlines the treatment and follow-up.

questions.stem: Which federal law primarily governs the privacy and security of protected health information held by covered healthcare entities?
questions.explanation: The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) sets national standards protecting the privacy and security of individually identifiable health information held by covered entities. FERPA governs the privacy of student education records, not general health records.

questions.stem: A standardized claim form used by healthcare providers to bill third-party payers for professional services is known as which of the following?
